}}
Adults have a striking black-and-white [[plumage]], with a black cap extending to the back of the neck.<ref name="OiseauxEurope">{{cite book |last1=Jiguet |first1=Frédéric |title=Tous les oiseaux d'Europe |last2=Audevard |first2=Aurélien |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2015 |isbn=978-2-603-02167-5 |location=Paris |page=149}}</ref> The [[Flight feather|primaries]] are black, with the base of the inner primaries white, while the secondary coverts are dark grey. The rectrices are pale grey-brown in the middle and white elsewhere.<ref name="geroudet">{{cite book |last=Géroudet |first=Paul |title=Limicoles, gangas et pigeons d'Europe |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2008 |isbn=978-2-603-01527-8 |page=61-69 |trans-title=Waders, Sandgrouse, and Pigeons of Europe}}</ref> The species shows no seasonal plumage variation. The tail is white, and the legs are bluish. The long, black [[beak]] is slender and upturned.<ref name="OiseauxEurope" />

[[Sexual dimorphism]] is subtle and difficult to discern in the field.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux">{{cite web |author=Inventaire National du Patrimoine Naturel |title=Cahiers d’Habitat « Oiseaux » - MEEDDAT- MNHN – Fiche projet |trans-title=Habitat Notebooks "Birds" - MEEDDAT- MNHN – Project Sheet |url=https://inpn.mnhn.fr/docs/cahab/fiches/Avocette-elegante.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150209031602/https://inpn.mnhn.fr/docs/cahab/fiches/Avocette-elegante.pdf |archive-date=9 February 2015 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> The male's bill is longer and less curved, while the female's is shorter and more distinctly curved.<ref name="Svensson">{{cite book |last=Svensson |first=Lars |title=Le guide ornitho |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2010 |isbn=978-2-603-01695-4 |location=Paris |page=134-135 |trans-title=The Bird Guide}}</ref> The black markings on the female's head may occasionally appear browner and less distinct.<ref name="Svensson" /> Males are generally larger than females, with a red or reddish-brown [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]], while females have a hazel-brown iris.<ref name="geroudet" />

In flight, the wingtips and shoulders are black, and the legs extend beyond the tail.<ref name="OiseauxEurope" />

Chicks are covered in pale grey-brown down, finely speckled with black, with black patterns on the head, four rows of black spots on the back, and a white underbelly tinged yellowish at the neck and belly. Their iris is dark brown.<ref name="geroudet" />

Juveniles have dingy grey wing coverts,<ref name="OiseauxEurope" /> with back and [[scapular]] feathers vermiculated with reddish-brown.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> Their legs are greyish.<ref name="Svensson" /> By their first summer, the primaries of juveniles appear very worn and brownish.<ref name="Svensson" /> After their first [[moult]], which occurs by late September, juveniles resemble adults.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

Two moults occur: a partial moult in February–March before breeding and a complete moult after breeding, from July to October.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

=== Measurements ===
The pied avocet measures between {{cvt|42|and|46|cm}} in length,<ref name="Svensson2">{{cite book |last=Svensson |first=Lars |title=Le guide ornitho |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2010 |isbn=978-2-603-01695-4 |location=Paris |page=134-135 |trans-title=The Bird Guide}}</ref> with a wingspan of {{cvt|67|to|77|cm}}, occasionally up to {{cvt|80|cm}}.<ref name="MNHNFicheDescription">{{cite web |author=Inventaire National du Patrimoine Naturel |title=Fiche descriptive de l’espèce |trans-title=Species Description Sheet |url=https://inpn.mnhn.fr/espece/cd_nom/3116/tab/fiche |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220109073609/https://inpn.mnhn.fr/espece/cd_nom/3116/tab/fiche |archive-date=9 January 2022 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> The wing of an adult male measures {{cvt|22|to|23.8|cm}}, while that of a female measures {{convert|21.9|to|24|cm|in}}.<ref name="demongin">{{cite book |last=Demongin |first=Laurent |title=Guide d'identification des oiseaux en main |year=2020 |isbn=978-2-9555019-1-7 |pages=542 |trans-title=Identification Guide to Birds in Hand}}</ref>

The bill measures {{cvt|8.2|to|9.1|cm}} in males and is slightly shorter in females, ranging from {{cvt|7.2|to|8.5|cm}}. The legs extend beyond {{cvt|10|cm}}, with a [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] of {{cvt|8.5|to|9.4|cm}} in males and {{cvt|7.5|to|9.2|cm}} in females.<ref name="Svensson2" /><ref name="demongin" /> The tail measures {{cvt|8|to|9|cm}} in adult males and {{cvt|7.8|to|8.7|cm}} in adult females.<ref name="demongin" />

The weight ranges from {{cvt|267|to|382|g}}, with an average of {{cvt|325|g}}.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

== Behaviour ==
This [[avocet]] forages in shallow brackish water or on mud flats, often scything its bill from side to side in water (a feeding technique that is unique to the avocets). It mainly eats [[crustacean]]s and [[insect]]s.<ref name="geroudet4">{{cite book |last=Géroudet |first=Paul |title=Limicoles, gangas et pigeons d'Europe |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2008 |isbn=978-2-603-01527-8 |page=61-69 |trans-title=Waders, Sandgrouse, and Pigeons of Europe}}</ref>

Its [[breeding in the wild|breeding]] [[habitat]] is shallow lakes with brackish water and exposed bare mud. It nests on open ground, often in small groups, sometimes with other waders. Three to five eggs are laid in a lined scrape or on a mound of vegetation.<ref name="geroudet3">{{cite book |last=Géroudet |first=Paul |title=Limicoles, gangas et pigeons d'Europe |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2008 |isbn=978-2-603-01527-8 |page=61-69 |trans-title=Waders, Sandgrouse, and Pigeons of Europe}}</ref>

=== Vocalizations ===
The contact and alarm calls, often emitted by adults when chicks are threatened, are a fluty whistle, sometimes plaintive, resembling ''plut-plut-plutt'' or ''klup-klup-klup''.<ref name="Svensson3">{{cite book |last=Svensson |first=Lars |title=Le guide ornitho |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2010 |isbn=978-2-603-01695-4 |location=Paris |page=134-135 |trans-title=The Bird Guide}}</ref> [[Paul Géroudet]] notes ''kvit-kvit-kvit'' when birds are excited, as well as ''krit-krit'' or ''kvèt''. Near predators, avocets may emit ''kriyu''. They also produce soft ''buk-buk'' during chick-rearing or ''glouglou... grrugrrugrru'' with mates during breeding.<ref name="geroudet2">{{cite book |last=Géroudet |first=Paul |title=Limicoles, gangas et pigeons d'Europe |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2008 |isbn=978-2-603-01527-8 |page=61-69 |trans-title=Waders, Sandgrouse, and Pigeons of Europe}}</ref>

=== Diet and feeding techniques ===
[[File:Pied_Avocet,_Recurvirostra_avosetta_at_Marievale_Nature_Reserve,_Gauteng,_South_Africa_(21024333882).jpg|alt=Photo of a bird swimming in water and eating a small invertebrate with its bill.|left|thumb|Feeding on small invertebrates with its curved bill.]]
The pied avocet primarily feeds on small [[Benthos|benthic]] invertebrates, such as [[Annelida|annelids]], small crustaceans like ''[[Corophium volutator|Corophium]]'', [[Oligochaeta|oligochaete]] and [[Polychaeta|polychaete]] worms (e.g., ''[[Hediste diversicolor]]'' or ''Polydora'' species), and [[Bivalvia|bivalve molluscs]].<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance">{{cite book |last1=Issa |first1=Nidal |title=Atlas des oiseaux de France métropolitaine |last2=Muller |first2=Yves |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2015 |isbn=978-2-603-01878-1 |location=Paris |page=498-501 |trans-title=Atlas of Breeding and Wintering Birds of Metropolitan France}}</ref><ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> It also consumes small insects (midges, beetles) measuring {{cvt|4|to|15|mm}}.<ref name="delhoyo">{{cite book |last1=del Hoyo |first1=J. |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World, vol. 3 |last2=Elliott |first2=A. |last3=Sargatal |first3=J. |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=1996 |isbn=978-84-87334-20-7 |pages=821}}</ref><ref name="johnsgard">{{cite book |last=Johnsgard |first=PA. |title=The plovers, sandpipers and snipes of the world |publisher=[[University of Nebraska Press]] |year=1981 |isbn=0-8032-2553-9 |pages=493}}</ref><ref name="geroudet2" /> Daily food intake averages {{cvt|150|g}} per individual.<ref name="geroudet2" />

[[Chironomidae|Chironomid]] larvae are a key food source during breeding and wintering in [[salt marsh]]es.<ref name="chironomes">{{cite journal |last1=Chépeau |first1=Y. |last2=Le Dréan-Quenec’hdu |first2=S. |year=1995 |title=Caractéristiques des sites d’alimentation nocturne des avocettes élégantes ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' dans la presqu’île guérandaise |trans-title=Characteristics of Nocturnal Feeding Sites of Pied Avocets ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in the Guérande Peninsula |journal=Alauda |volume=63 |pages=169-178}}</ref>

It occasionally eats small fish, seeds, and small roots.<ref name="delhoyo" /><ref name="urban">{{cite book |last1=Urban |first1=EK. |title=The Birds of Africa, Volume II |last2=Fry |first2=CH. |last3=Keith |first3=S. |publisher=[[Academic Press]] |year=1986 |isbn=0-12-137302-9 |location=London/Orlando/San Diego |pages=552}}</ref>

It uses its bill to probe the [[sediment]] surface, making lateral pecks to find prey, or catches them by sight.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name="geroudet2" /> In group feeding, lateral pecks are faster and nearly continuous.<ref name="geroudet2" /> Adults feed in water depths of {{cvt|4|to|10|cm}}.<ref name="epaisseureau">{{cite journal |last1=Claireaux |first1=P. |last2=Yésou |first2=P. |year=1986 |title=Utilisation d’un marais saumâtre par l’Avocette ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' L. : influence de quelques paramètres du milieu |trans-title=Use of a Brackish Marsh by the Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' L.: Influence of Environmental Parameters |journal=Gibier Faune Sauvage |volume=3 |pages=97-113}}</ref> It may also peck on beaches or swim in deeper water, dipping its head like a duck.<ref name="Svensson3" /> In groups, it may extend its neck, sweep its bill through the mud, and repeat the motion.<ref name="geroudet2" />

Locally, it may align its [[circadian rhythm]] with tides, feeding at night in salt marshes, such as in the [[Guérande Peninsula]].<ref name="chironomes" />

=== Philopatry ===
The pied avocet is [[philopatric]]. This has been demonstrated through banding studies in Atlantic sites like the [[Gulf of Morbihan]], Guérande Marshes, and Müllembourg Marshes on Noirmoutier Island, where some banded birds returned to nest.<ref name="avocettehyeres">{{cite journal |last=Audevard |first=A. |year=2019 |title=Bilan ornithologique des Salins d’Hyères pour l’année 2017 |trans-title=Ornithological Report of the Hyères Salt Flats for 2017 |url=https://cdnfiles1.biolovision.net/www.faune-paca.org/userfiles/FPPubli/FPP84Bilanornithologique2017dessalinsdHyres.pdf |journal=Faune-PACA Publication |issue=84 |pages=87 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> Survival and return rates in the Atlantic range from 48–75% (average 58%) for first-year birds and 78–100% (average 90%) for adults, depending on the year.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

Philopatry may explain increased breeding populations at some sites, as many new breeders are offspring from previous seasons.<ref name="avocettehyeres" /> When sites reach capacity, new colonies form elsewhere, boosting populations.<ref name="hill">{{cite journal |last=Hill |first=D. |year=1988 |title=Population dynamics of the Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' breeding in Britain |journal=J. Anim. Ecol. |volume=57 |pages=669-683}}</ref>

=== Reproduction ===

==== Nest and eggs ====
[[File:Recurvirostra_avosetta_MHNT.JPG|alt=Photo of a beige egg speckled with black.|right|thumb|Egg from the [[Muséum de Toulouse|Toulouse Museum]] collection.]]
The species is semi-colonial.<ref name="semicolo">{{cite journal |last1=Girard |first1=O. |last2=Yésou |first2=P. |year=1991 |title=Développement spatial d’une colonie d’avocettes (''Recurvirostra avocetta'') |trans-title=Spatial Development of an Avocet Colony (''Recurvirostra avosetta'') |journal=Gibier Faune Sauvage |volume=8 |pages=31-42}}</ref> It nests in dense colonies of 10–70 pairs,<ref name="johnsgard" /> sometimes up to 200 in multiple groups,<ref name="geroudet2" /> or as isolated pairs. In Algeria, islets with 798 nests have been recorded.<ref name="menouar">{{cite journal |last1=Menouar |first1=S. |last2=Yacine |first2=N. |last3=Abdellaziz |first3=B. |last4=Ettayib |first4=B. |last5=Boudjema |first5=S. |last6=Moussa |first6=H. |year=2009 |title=Ecologie de la reproduction de l’Avocette élégante ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' dans la Garaet de Guellif (Hautes plaines de l’Est algérien) |trans-title=Reproductive Ecology of the Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in the Guellif Garaet (Eastern Algerian Highlands) |journal=European Journal of Scientific Research |issue=25 |pages=513-525}}</ref> It is [[monogamous]], with pairs forming soon after arriving at breeding sites.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> It often nests in mixed colonies with [[Laridae|gulls]] and waders, especially [[tern]]s.<ref name="geroudet2" />

Pairs perform rituals to bond, such as tossing debris, pretending to drink, or bowing side by side.<ref name="geroudet2" /> Mating involves the male preening after dipping its bill in water, while the female leans forward. The male moves around the female, repeatedly dipping its bill. During copulation, the female sways her neck laterally.<ref name="geroudet2" /> Post-copulation, the pair crosses bills, and the male drapes his wing over the female before separating.<ref name="geroudet2" />

Some males have been observed attempting to mate with inanimate objects.<ref name="accouplementobjet">{{cite journal |last=Hamilton |first=R. |year=1975 |title=Comparative Behavior of the American Avocet and the Black-Necked Stilt (Recurvirostridae) |journal=Ornithological Monographs |issue=17}}</ref>

The nest is a shallow scrape, averaging {{cvt|12|to|12.55|cm}} in diameter, ranging from {{cvt|6|to|22|cm}},<ref name="menouar" /> and {{convert|3|to|4|cm|in}} deep.<ref name="geroudet2" /> It is placed near water,<ref name="Svensson3" /> up to {{cvt|15|cm|}} from the shore, preferably on islets or dikes, in sand, short vegetation, or debris,<ref name="urban" /><ref name="johnsgard" /> but never in mud.<ref name="bouealgerie">{{cite journal |last1=Baaziz |first1=N. |last2=Mayache |first2=B. |last3=Saheb |first3=M. |last4=Bensaci |first4=E. |last5=Ounissi |first5=M. |last6=Metallaoui |first6=S. |last7=Houhamdi |first7=M. |year=2011 |title=Statut phénologique et reproduction des peuplements d’oiseaux d’eau dans l’éco-complexe de zones humides de Sétif (Hauts plateaux, Est de l’Algérie) |trans-title=Phenological Status and Reproduction of Waterbird Populations in the Wetland Eco-Complex of Sétif (High Plateaus, Eastern Algeria) |journal=Bulletin de l’Institut Scientifique de Rabat |issue=32 |pages=77-87}}</ref><ref name="boueiran">{{cite journal |last1=Barati |first1=A. |last2=Nouri |first2=S. V. |year=2009 |title=Breeding ecology of the Pied Avocet Recurvirostra avosetta in Agh Gol wetland, Hamedan Province, Iran |journal=Avosetta |issue=33 |pages=43-47}}</ref> Islets improve hatching success by reducing predation.<ref name="lengyel">{{cite journal |last=Lengyel |first=S. |year=2006 |title=Spatial differences in breeding success in the pied avocet Recurvirostra avosetta: effects of habitat on hatching success and chick survival |journal=Journal of Avian Biology |issue=37 |pages=381-395}}</ref> Unlike the [[Eurasian oystercatcher]], the avocet does not adjust nest size for water level changes.<ref name="cuervo">{{cite journal |last=Cuervo |first=J. J. |year=2004 |title=Nest-site selection and characteristics in a mixed-species colony of Avocets ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' and Black-winged Stilts ''Himantopus himantopus'' |journal=Bird Study |issue=51 |pages=20-24}}</ref>

Nests often include shell fragments, particularly ''[[Cerastoderma glaucum]]'', and vegetation like samphire, [[Poaceae|grasses]], [[Chenopodiaceae|goosefoots]], [[Limonium|sea-lavender]], [[Bromus|bromes]], [[Ruppia maritima|widgeon grass]], or [[Bolboschoenus maritimus|saltmarsh bulrush]].<ref name="plumenaturaliste">{{cite journal |last1=Laurent |first1=S. |last2=Brenon |first2=D. |last3=Kraemer |first3=P. |last4=Triplet |first4=P. |year=2020 |title=Caractérisation des habitats et des îlots utilisés par l’Avocette élégante (''Recurvirostra avosetta''), l’Huîtrier pie (''Haematopus ostralegus''), la Mouette rieuse (''Chroicocephalus ridibundus''), la Mouette mélanocéphale (''Ichthyaetus melanocephalus'') et la Sterne caugek (''Thalasseus sandvicensis'') en période de reproduction |trans-title=Characterization of Habitats and Islets Used by the Pied Avocet (''Recurvirostra avosetta''), Eurasian Oystercatcher (''Haematopus ostralegus''), Black-headed Gull (''Chroicocephalus ridibundus''), Mediterranean Gull (''Ichthyaetus melanocephalus''), and Sandwich Tern (''Thalasseus sandvicensis'') During Breeding |url=http://www.plume-de-naturalistes.fr/wp-content/uploads/2020/02/3_LAURENT-et-al_02-2020_Habitats-de-reproduction-de-5-laro-limicoles_Plume-4_43-60..pdf |journal=Plume de Naturalistes |issue=4 |pages=43-60 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref>

In colonies, nests are typically spaced {{cvt|20|cm}} to {{cvt|1|m|ft}}, though in an Iranian colony, the average was {{cvt|2.74|m|ft}}.<ref name="urban" /><ref name="hayman">{{cite book |last1=Hayman |first1=P. |title=Shorebirds. An Identification Guide to the Waders of the World |last2=Marchant |first2=J. |last3=Prater |first3=A. J. |year=1986}}</ref><ref name="boueiran" />

Egg-laying occurs from early April to early July,<ref name="avocettehyeres" /> peaking from mid-April to mid-May.<ref name="ponte">{{cite journal |last1=Girard |first1=O. |last2=Yésou |first2=P. |year=1989 |title=Reproduction de l’avocette (''Recurvirostra avocetta'') sur le marais d’Olonne : chronologie et devenir des pontes |trans-title=Reproduction of the Pied Avocet (''Recurvirostra avosetta'') in the Olonne Marsh: Chronology and Fate of Clutches |journal=Gibier Faune Sauvage |volume=6 |pages=225-243}}</ref> Clutches range from 69 to 92 days,<ref name="ponte" /> typically with three to five eggs, occasionally more due to intraspecific parasitism.<ref name="avocettehyeres" /><ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name="parasitisme">{{cite journal |last=Hötker |first=H. |year=2000 |title=Conspecific nest parasitism in the pied avocet Recurvirostra avosetta |journal=Ibis |volume=142 |pages=280-288}}</ref> Eggs measure {{cvt|55|by|35|mm}} and weigh about {{cvt|31.7|g}}.<ref name="BTO">{{cite journal |last=Robinson |first=R.A. |year=2005 |title=Avocet Recurvirostra avosetta |url=https://app.bto.org/birdfacts/results/bob4560.htm |journal=BTO |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191203182617/https://app.bto.org/birdfacts/results/bob4560.htm |archive-date=3 December 2019 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> Incubation lasts 19–34 days, averaging 23 days.<ref name="incubation">{{cite journal |last=Hötker |first=H. |year=1998 |title=Intraspecific variation in length of incubation period in avocets ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' |journal=Ardea |volume=86 |issue=1 |pages=33-41}}</ref> Both parents incubate, with a ritual involving debris-tossing and sliding under the incubating bird to take over. This ritual diminishes over time. The non-incubating parent may feed up to {{cvt|4|km}} from the colony.<ref name="geroudet2" />

The species typically lays one clutch per year, with replacement clutches if eggs are lost, usually to predation. Hatching success varies by site and year, ranging from 54–78% in the Olonne Marsh<ref name="ponte" /> and 8–59% in the Séné Marshes.<ref name="ponte" />

==== Chick rearing and productivity ====
[[File:Pied_Avocet_chick.jpg|alt=Photo of a chick approaching water on a beach, with its reflection in the water.|left|thumb|Chick, a few weeks old.]]
Chicks are [[nidifugous]], leaving the nest after the last egg hatches.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> They remain in the nest for at least five hours post-hatching.<ref name="stanescu">{{cite journal |last=Stanescu |first=D. |year=1976 |title=Agresivitate fata de pui la ''Recurvirostra avocetta'' |trans-title=Aggressiveness Towards Chicks in ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' |journal=Studii si Comunicari |volume=20 |pages=319-325}}</ref> In the first hours, they alternate between sheltering under parents and exploring nearby.<ref name="adret2">{{cite journal |last=Adret |first=P. |year=2018 |title=Une étude des relations spatiales entre jeunes avocettes sur leurs lieux d'élevage |trans-title=A Study of Spatial Relationships Among Young Avocets on Their Rearing Grounds |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/326957290_Une_etude_des_relations_spatiales_entre_jeunes_avocettes_sur_leurs_lieux_d'elevage_Translated_as_Spatial_organization_in_broods_of_young_avocets_on_their_rearing_grounds |journal=Revue d'Ecologie |volume=39 |pages=193-208 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> Within 72 hours, the family moves to nearby feeding areas.

Both parents protect chicks from predators and weather.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> The species is highly territorial during chick-rearing, defending feeding and rearing areas against other birds.<ref name="adret">{{cite journal |last=Adret |first=P. |year=1983 |title=Une étude des comportements parentaux de l’avocette en colonie de reproduction. Organisation spatiale inter- et intrafamiliale |trans-title=A Study of Parental Behaviors of the Avocet in a Breeding Colony: Inter- and Intrafamily Spatial Organization |url=https://d1wqtxts1xzle7.cloudfront.net/31471647/Adret-CanJZool-1983.pdf |journal=Canadian Journal of Zoology |volume=61 |pages=603-615 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> Adults use distraction displays, such as feigning a broken wing or swooping at intruders.<ref name="geroudet2" /> Chicks hide in samphire, flee across mudflats, or swim when threatened.

Each family has a stable rearing territory, proportional to chick number, ranging from {{cvt|260|to|5200|m2}}.<ref name="adret" /> Stability depends on food and space availability. Asynchronous hatching may optimize mudflat use.<ref name="adret" />

Chicks feed in exposed mudflats or water up to {{cvt|1.5|cm}} deep. They may travel significant distances shortly after hatching.<ref name="epaisseureau" />

Chicks fledge and become independent 35–42 days after hatching.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> Rarely, chicks are raised by other species, such as [[Eurasian Oystercatcher|Eurasian Oystercatchers]] in Somme Bay in 1981, where a chick was fed for two months, adopting oystercatcher behaviors.<ref name="huitrier">{{cite journal |last1=Triplet |first1=P. |last2=Sueur |first2=F. |year=1983 |title=Elevage d’une jeune Avocette ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' par un couple d’Huîtriers pies ''Haematopus ostralegus'' dans le Marquenterre (Somme) |trans-title=Rearing of a Young Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' by a Pair of Eurasian Oystercatchers ''Haematopus ostralegus'' in Marquenterre (Somme) |url=https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Patrick_Triplet/publication/282910155_Elevage_d%27une_jeune_Avocette_Recurvirostra_avosetta_par_un_couple_d%27Huitriers-pies_Haematopus_ostralegus_dans_le_Marquenterre/links/5622444a08aed8dd1943ef08/Elevage-dune-jeune-Avocette-Recurvirostra-avosetta-par-un-couple-dHuitriers-pies-Haematopus-ostralegus-dans-le-Marquenterre.pdf |journal=L’Oiseau et la Revue Française d’Ornithologie |volume=53 |pages=251-260 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref>

Reproductive success varies by year and site, with productivity of 0.49–0.52 fledglings per pair on the Atlantic and Channel coasts,<ref name="vanimpe">{{cite journal |last=Van Himpe |first=J. |year=1991 |title=Overleving, Sterfte en Trek van in België geringde jonge Kluten ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' |trans-title=Survival, Mortality, and Migration of Young Avocets Banded in Belgium ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' |journal=Gerfaut |volume=81 |pages=217-243}}</ref><ref name="avosomme">{{cite journal |last1=Triplet |first1=P. |last2=Carruette |first2=P. |year=1996 |title=Restauration d’une colonie d’Avocettes en baie de Somme (Parc Ornithologique du Marquenterre, Réserve Naturelle de la Baie de Somme) |trans-title=Restoration of an Avocet Colony in Somme Bay (Marquenterre Ornithological Park, Somme Bay National Nature Reserve) |url=https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Patrick_Triplet/publication/282910449_Restauration_d%27une_colonie_d%27Avocettes_en_baie_de_Somme_Parc_Ornithologique_du_Marquenterre_Reserve_Naturelle_de_la_Baie_de_Somme/links/5622480d08aea35f2681c38e.pdf |journal=Avifaune picarde |volume=2 |pages=82-90 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> 0.45–0.63 in Languedoc colonies, and 0.04–0.22 in [[Camargue]].<ref name="repromedit">{{cite journal |last1=Sadoul |first1=N. |last2=Cramm |first2=P. |last3=Orsini |first3=P. |year=2005 |title=Population trends, reproduction and conservation issues of the Avocet breeding on the Mediterranean coast of France |journal=Wader Study Group Bulletin |volume=107 |pages=94-97}}</ref>

Sexual maturity varies: 25% of French birds breed in their first year, 40% in their second, while North Sea birds mature between two and five years.<ref name="maturite">{{cite journal |last=Hötker |first=H. |year=2002 |title=Arrival of pied avocets ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' at the breeding site: effects of winter quarters and consequences for reproductive success |journal=Ardea |volume=90 |pages=379-387}}</ref><ref name="maturite2">{{cite journal |last1=Cadbury |first1=C.J. |last2=Olney |first2=P.J.S. |year=1978 |title=Arrival Avocet population dynamics in England |journal=British Birds |volume=71 |pages=102-121}}</ref> Generally, maturity is reached at 2–3 years.<ref name="geroudet2" />

First-year mortality is 50–60%, dropping to 20–30% in subsequent years.<ref name="geroudet2" /> The maximum recorded lifespan for a wild banded bird is 27 years,<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> with another observed nearly 25 years post-banding,<ref name="BTO" /> though the average is 12 years.<ref name="geroudet2" />

==== Reproductive failure factors ====
The main causes of reproductive failure are intraspecific conflicts<ref name="echecintra">{{cite journal |last1=Sueur |first1=F. |last2=Triplet |first2=P. |last3=Carruette |first3=P. |year=2007 |title=Trente ans de reproduction de l'Avocette élégante Recurvirostra avosetta dans le Parc du Marquenterre (Réserve Naturelle de la Baie de Somme) |trans-title=Thirty Years of Reproduction of the Pied Avocet Recurvirostra avosetta in Marquenterre Park (Somme Bay National Nature Reserve) |journal=Alauda |volume=75 |pages=45-50}}</ref><ref name="repromedit" /> and predation by other species.

Predators include corvids like the [[carrion crow]] (''Corvus corone'') and [[common magpie]] (''Pica pica''), gulls such as the [[European herring gull]] (''Larus argentatus'') in the Atlantic<ref name="avosomme" /> and [[yellow-legged gull]] (''Larus michahellis'') in the Mediterranean, and raptors, particularly the [[common kestrel]] (''Falco tinnunculus''), which targets chicks.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name="avocettehyeres" /> The [[peregrine falcon]] (''Falco peregrinus'') preys on adults, while the [[western marsh harrier]] (''Circus aeruginosus'') and [[white stork]] (''Ciconia ciconia'') may take chicks.<ref name="adret2" /><ref name="echecintra" /> Mammalian predators include the [[red fox]] (''Vulpes vulpes''), possibly the [[wild boar]] (''Sus scrofa''), [[Mustelidae|mustelids]], the [[brown rat]] (''Rattus norvegicus''), and stray dogs.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name="avocettehyeres" /> Increased gull populations can disrupt colonies.<ref name="provence">{{cite book |last1=Lascève |first1=M. |title=Oiseaux remarquables de Provence. Ecologie, statut et conservation |last2=Crocq |first2=C. |last3=Kabouche |first3=B. |last4=Flitti |first4=A. |last5=Dhermain |first5=F. |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2006 |isbn=2-603-01391-2 |location=Lonay (Switzerland)/Paris |pages=317 |trans-title=Remarkable Birds of Provence: Ecology, Status, and Conservation}}</ref>

Nests may be destroyed by flooding<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> or trampled by livestock.<ref name="goutner">{{cite journal |last=Goutner |first=V. |year=1985 |title=Breeding ecology of the avocet (''Recurvirostra avosetta'' L.) in the Evros delta (Greece) |journal=Bonn. Zool. Beitr |issue=36 |pages=37-50}}</ref> Poor [[water quality]] or inappropriate salinity can limit food availability,<ref name="avosomme" /><ref name="hill" /> as can excessive salinity.<ref name="hill" />

Spatial competition with other gull-wader species, such as the [[black-headed gull]] (''Chroicocephalus ridibundus''), [[Mediterranean gull]] (''Ichthyaetus melanocephalus''), [[European herring gull]], [[gull-billed tern]] (''Gelochelidon nilotica''), [[sandwich tern]] (''Thalasseus sandvicensis''), [[common tern]] (''Sterna hirundo''), and [[little tern]] (''Sternula albifrons''), can hinder breeding.<ref name="goutner" /><ref name="italy">{{cite journal |last1=Tinarelli |first1=R. |last2=Baccetti |first2=N. |year=1989 |title=Breeding waders in Italy |journal=Wader Study Group Bull |issue=56 |pages=7-15}}</ref>

Chick dispersal for food increases mortality risk,<ref name="hill" /><ref name="bouche">{{cite journal |last=Bouche |first=S. |year=1991 |title=Partage des ressources et succès reproducteur d'une colonie d'Avocettes à manteau noir ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' |trans-title=Resource Sharing and Reproductive Success of a Black-Mantled Avocet Colony ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' |journal=Alauda |volume=59 |pages=38-39}}</ref> and harsh weather affects chicks aged two to three weeks.<ref name="avosomme" />

== Parasites ==
The species hosts parasites like ''[[Giardia]]''<ref name="giardia">{{cite journal |last=Lavier |first=G. |year=1923 |title=Sur deux espèces nouvelles du genre Giardia: G. viscaciæ de la viscache (Viscacia viscacia) et G. varani du varan (Varanus niloticus) |trans-title=On Two New Species of the Genus Giardia: G. viscaciæ of the Viscacha (Viscacia viscacia) and G. varani of the Monitor Lizard (Varanus niloticus) |url=https://www.parasite-journal.org/articles/parasite/pdf/1923/02/parasite1923012p147.pdf |journal=Annales de Parasitologie Humaine et Comparée |volume=1 |issue=2 |pages=147-154 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> and ''Eurycestus avoceti'', first observed in the [[American avocet]] and later in [[Camargue]] individuals, with the crustacean ''[[Artemia salina]]'' as an intermediate host.<ref name="Eurycestus1">{{cite journal |last1=Robert |first1=F. |last2=Gabrion |first2=C. |year=1991 |title=Cestodoses de l’avifaune camarguaise. Rôle d’Artemia (Crustacea, Anostraca) et stratégies de rencontre hôte-parasite |trans-title=Cestodiasis in Camargue Avifauna: Role of Artemia (Crustacea, Anostraca) and Host-Parasite Encounter Strategies |url=https://www.parasite-journal.org/articles/parasite/pdf/1991/05/parasite1991665p226.pdf |journal=Annales de Parasitologie Humaine et Comparée |volume=66 |issue=5 |pages=226-235 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref><ref name="Eurycestus2">{{cite journal |last1=Gabrion |first1=C. |last2=Mac Donald |first2=G. |year=1991 |title=''Artemia'' sp.(Crustacé, Anostracé), hôte intermédiaire d’''Eurycestus avoceti'' Clark, 1954 (Cestode Cyclophyllide)-parasite de l’avocette en Camargue |trans-title=''Artemia'' sp. (Crustacean, Anostracan), Intermediate Host of ''Eurycestus avoceti'' Clark, 1954 (Cyclophyllidean Cestode) - Parasite of the Avocet in the Camargue |url=https://www.parasite-journal.org/articles/parasite/pdf/1980/03/parasite1980553p327.pdf |journal=Annales de Parasitologie Humaine et Comparée |volume=55 |issue=3 |pages=327-331 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref>

== Habitats ==
[[File:Salins_de_Frontignan_03.jpg|alt=Color photo taken by a pond, with houses in the background and a dirt path on the side.|right|thumb|Typical Mediterranean habitat, here at the Frontignan Salt Flats in Hérault.]]
The species frequents coastal habitats. In winter, it gathers in groups on soft substrates like brackish or saline bays, sandbanks, [[Estuary|estuaries]], lagoons, deltas, or [[intertidal]] mudflats, used for resting and feeding.<ref name="Svensson3" /><ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance" /> During high tides, it may stay in water or gather on [[salt marsh]]es or [[Spartina maritima|cordgrass]] meadows.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> It also occurs in shallow lakes, ponds, salt marshes, or various floodplains, rarely on inland lakes or rivers, and sometimes feeds on agricultural land.<ref name="delhoyo" /><ref name="urban" />

During breeding, Mediterranean populations use similar habitats.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> On the Atlantic coast, it inhabits wetlands, natural or artificial, with sparse vegetation and shallow water pockets with mud, sand, or clay islets,<ref name="snow">{{cite book |last1=Snow |first1=DW. |title=The Birds of the Western Palearctic, Volume 1 |last2=Perrins |first2=CM. |publisher=[[Oxford University Press]] |year=1998 |isbn=0-19-854099-X |pages=1832}}</ref> or dikes with samphire.<ref name="sansouire">{{cite book |last1=Bensettiti |first1=F. |title=« Cahiers d’habitats » Natura 2000. Connaissance et gestion des habitats et des espèces d’intérêt communautaire. Tome 2 |last2=Bioret |first2=F. |last3=Roland |first3=J. |last4=Lacoste |first4=J.-P. |publisher=MEDD/MAAPAR/MNHN, La Documentation française |year=2004 |pages=399 |trans-title=Natura 2000 Habitat Notebooks: Knowledge and Management of Habitats and Species of Community Interest, Volume 2}}</ref> Preferred islets are elongated, {{cvt|3|to|15|m|0}} long and {{cvt|3|to|4|m|0}} wide, offering a clear view of nearby water.<ref name="avosomme" />

Locally, it may occupy port facilities, such as in Somme Bay,<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> former [[polder]]s, or sugar refinery [[settling basin]]s in northern France.<ref name="sucrerie">{{cite journal |last=Larousse |first=A. |year=1995 |title=Nidification de l’avocette ''Recurvirostra avocetta'' dans le centre de la France |trans-title=Nesting of the Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in Central France |journal=Ornithos |volume=2 |pages=139-141}}</ref> It has been observed breeding near hunting blinds.<ref name="huttechasse">{{cite journal |last1=Schricke |first1=V. |last2=Desmidt |first2=Y. |last3=Guerin |first3=D. |year=1999 |title=Premier cas de nidification de l’Avocette élégante ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' en baie du Mont Saint-Michel |trans-title=First Case of Nesting of the Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in Mont Saint-Michel Bay |journal=Alauda |volume=67 |issue=1 |pages=14}}</ref> It breeds up to {{cvt|1000|m}} in altitude in [[Anatolia]].<ref name="geroudet2" />

Key breeding habitat features include gradually decreasing water levels to expose feeding areas and high salinity to limit vegetation growth.<ref name="johnsgard" />

The [[European Nature Information System|EUNIS]] lists brackish and saline coastal lagoons and estuaries as key habitats.<ref name="EUNIS">{{cite book |last=Trouvilliez |first=J. |title=« Cahiers d'habitats » Natura 2000. Connaissance et gestion des habitats et des espèces d'intérêt communautaire. Tome 8 |publisher=MEDD/MAAPAR/MNHN, La Documentation française |year=2012 |trans-title=Natura 2000 Habitat Notebooks: Knowledge and Management of Habitats and Species of Community Interest, Volume 8}}</ref>

== Distribution ==
The pied avocet is a Turano-Mediterranean species, found in the [[Palearctic]]. Its fragmented range spans European coastlines from southern [[Scandinavia]] and the [[Baltic states]] (e.g., [[Estonia]] since 1964) to the [[Iberian Peninsula]], the Mediterranean, [[Black Sea]], and eastern European plains ([[Ukraine]], [[Bulgaria]], [[Romania]], [[Hungary]]).<ref name="delhoyo2">{{cite book |last1=del Hoyo |first1=J. |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World, vol. 3 |last2=Elliott |first2=A. |last3=Sargatal |first3=J. |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=1996 |isbn=978-84-87334-20-7 |pages=821}}</ref><ref name="hayman2">{{cite book |last1=Hayman |first1=P. |title=Shorebirds. An Identification Guide to the Waders of the World |last2=Marchant |first2=J. |last3=Prater |first3=A. J. |year=1986}}</ref> Major European populations are in the Netherlands, Germany, [[Denmark]], Spain (southern and [[Ebro Delta]]), Italy ([[Po Delta]] and [[Sardinia]]), France, and Russia.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2">{{cite book |last1=Issa |first1=Nidal |title=Atlas des oiseaux de France métropolitaine |last2=Muller |first2=Yves |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2015 |isbn=978-2-603-01878-1 |location=Paris |page=498-501 |trans-title=Atlas of Breeding and Wintering Birds of Metropolitan France}}</ref> It occurs on southern England coasts (about 150 pairs in 1979),<ref name="geroudet5">{{cite book |last=Géroudet |first=Paul |title=Limicoles, gangas et pigeons d'Europe |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2008 |isbn=978-2-603-01527-8 |page=61-69 |trans-title=Waders, Sandgrouse, and Pigeons of Europe}}</ref> having returned to breed in 1947 in Suffolk’s [[Minsmere RSPB reserve]] after disappearing in the mid-19th century.<ref name="MondeAnimal">{{cite book |last=von Westernhagen |first=Wolfgang |title=Le Monde animal en 13 volumes |publisher=Éditions Stauffacher S.A. |year=1974 |editor=Grzimek |editor-first=Bernhard |volume=VIII |page=172-173 |trans-title=The Animal World in 13 Volumes |chapter=VII - Limicoles |editor-last2=Fontaine |editor-first2=Maurice}}</ref><ref name="Chants">{{cite book |last1=Jiguet |first1=Frédéric |title=Chants d'Oiseaux |last2=Pedersen |first2=Jan |last3=Svensson |first3=Lars |publisher=[[Éditions Larousse]] |year=2012 |isbn=978-2-03-587204-3 |page=60-61 |trans-title=Bird Songs}}</ref>

In Asia, it ranges from [[Turkey]] (Anatolia) to northern [[Mongolia]], northern [[China]], southern [[Russian Far East]], [[Kazakhstan]], and Middle Eastern countries ([[Iran]], [[Iraq]], [[Jordan]]).<ref name="geroudet5" /> It is patchily distributed in the [[Arab states of the Persian Gulf|Gulf states]], [[Pakistan]], and the Indian subcontinent.<ref name="iucn"/>

In Africa, it occurs along the Mediterranean coast of North Africa, the [[Red Sea]], and [[Atlantic Ocean|Atlantic]] coast to [[Senegal]] and northern [[Guinea]], covering parts of the [[Sahel]], East Africa, and southern Africa.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name="iucn"/>

Northern European (Netherlands to Scandinavia) and eastern Asian populations are [[Bird migration|migratory]].<ref name="Svensson4">{{cite book |last=Svensson |first=Lars |title=Le guide ornitho |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2010 |isbn=978-2-603-01695-4 |location=Paris |page=134-135 |trans-title=The Bird Guide}}</ref> Western European birds winter in West Africa ([[Senegal]], [[Gambia]], [[Niger]]) and Mediterranean countries, especially Spain and [[Portugal]] (one-third of migratory European birds).<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> Eastern European birds winter in Niger, [[Chad]], [[Sudan]], and the Red Sea.<ref name="geroudet5" /> Some overwinter from Denmark to the Netherlands, England to Ireland.<ref name="geroudet5" /> Asian populations winter in East Africa or the Indian subcontinent.<ref name="iucn"/> Migratory groups of 5–30 birds move from August to October and return between March and May.<ref name="urban2">{{cite book |last1=Urban |first1=EK. |title=The Birds of Africa, Volume II |last2=Fry |first2=CH. |last3=Keith |first3=S. |publisher=[[Academic Press]] |year=1986 |isbn=0-12-137302-9 |location=London/Orlando/San Diego |pages=552}}</ref><ref name="delhoyo2" />

Mediterranean, some African, and Gulf populations are [[sedentary]].<ref name="iucn"/><ref name="delhoyo2" /> Some Camargue birds winter in [[Morocco]] and [[Tunisia]].<ref name="geroudet5" />

During migration, transalpine flights occur (e.g., in [[Engadin|Engadine]]), but inland sightings are rare. Individuals have been recorded as far as the [[Faroe Islands]], [[Azores]], and [[Canary Islands]].<ref name="geroudet5" />

=== In France ===

==== Historical presence ====
[[File:Saverio_Manetti,_Ornithologia_methodice_digesta,_per_monchiani,_firenze_1767-76,_024_avosetta_o_becco_storto.jpg|alt=Pencil drawing of a black-and-white bird with a curved bill in flight.|thumb|Illustration in [[Florence]], [[Italy]], by [[Saverio Manetti]] in the [[1770s]].]]
The Pied Avocet was present in France during the [[Roman Gaul]] period, evidenced by bones found in a waste pit in [[Lattes, Hérault|Lattes]], [[Hérault]], alongside other bird species.<ref name="oslattes">{{cite journal |last=Garcia |first=Lluís |year=2005 |title=Les restes d’oiseaux provenant des puits de Lattes |trans-title=Bird Remains from the Wells of Lattes |url=http://syslat.on-rev.com/ArcheOnLine/RAP/RAP000011.pdf |journal=Lattara |volume=18 |pages=271-275 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref> Later, a single specimen was found in 14th-century deposits in [[Abbeville]], [[Somme (department)|Somme]].<ref name="restessomme">{{cite journal |last=Clavel |first=Benoît |year=1997 |title=Les restes osseux animaux du Moyen Age décou verts place de l'Hôtel de ville à Abbeville (Somme) |trans-title=Animal Bone Remains from the Middle Ages Discovered at Abbeville Town Hall Square (Somme) |url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/pica_0752-5656_1997_num_3_1_2258 |journal=Revue archéologique de Picardie |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=193-205 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref>

It was noted as breeding in Camargue and [[Languedoc]] only from the early 20th century.<ref name="Mayaud">{{cite book |last=Mayaud |first=Noël |title=Inventaire des oiseaux de France |publisher=Société d'études ornithologiques Blot éditeur |year=1936 |pages=211 |trans-title=Inventory of the Birds of France}}</ref> It established breeding on the Atlantic coast from the 1950s, starting in [[Vendée]] in 1952, reaching [[Morbihan]] by 1983, and the [[English Channel]] and Somme Bay in the 1970s.<ref name="fichehistorique">{{cite journal |last1=Pascal |first1=Michel |last2=Lorvelec |first2=Olivier |last3=Vigne |first3=Jean-Denis |year=2003 |title=Evolution holocène de la faune de Vertébrés de France: invasions et extinctions |trans-title=Holocene Evolution of France’s Vertebrate Fauna: Invasions and Extinctions |url=https://inpn.mnhn.fr/docs-web/docs/download/194802 |journal=Institut National de Recherche Agronomique, Centre National de Recherche Scientifique, Muséum National d'Histoire Naturelle |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220109073607/https://inpn.mnhn.fr/docs-web/docs/download/194802 |archive-date=9 January 2022 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref><ref name="Yeatman">{{cite book |last1=Yeatman-Berthelot |first1=D. |title=Nouvel atlas des oiseaux nicheurs de France |last2=Jarry |first2=G. |publisher=Société Ornithologique de France |year=1994 |isbn=2-9505440-2-9 |location=Paris |page=266-267 |trans-title=New Atlas of Breeding Birds of France}}</ref> It likely bred in France before the 20th century, possibly colonizing or recolonizing spontaneously from its Mediterranean range.<ref name="fichehistorique" />

==== Current presence ====
The species is present year-round in France. In winter, northern migratory populations bolster local numbers, primarily along the Atlantic coast from the [[Gironde Estuary]] to [[Morbihan]], hosting 80–90% of the national wintering population.<ref name="pophiverfrance">{{cite journal |last1=Mahéo |first1=R. |last2=Le Dréan Quénéc'hdu |first2=S. |last3=Triplet |first3=P. |year=2007 |title=L'Avocette élégante ''Recurvirostra avocetta'' hivernant en France (littoral Manche-Atlantique), 1977-2005 |trans-title=The Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' Wintering in France (Channel-Atlantic Coast), 1977-2005 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282910134_L%27Avocette_elegante_Recurvirostra_avosetta_hivernant_en_France_Littoral_Manche-Atlantique_1977-2005 |journal=Alauda |volume=75 |pages=51-62 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref><ref name="hivernageavocette">{{cite journal |last1=Le Dréan Quénéc'hdu |first1=S. |last2=Triplet |first2=P. |last3=Mahéo |first3=R. |year=2007 |title=Caractéristiques des sites d'hivernage de l'Avocette élégante ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' en France : résultats de l'enquête 2002 |trans-title=Characteristics of Wintering Sites of the Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in France: Results of the 2002 Survey |journal=Alauda |volume=75 |pages=79-86}}</ref> It is less common along the English Channel, mainly in Somme Bay and [[Lower Normandy]],<ref name="avocetteseine">{{cite journal |last1=Aulert |first1=C. |last2=Hémery |first2=D. |year=2007 |title=L'Avocette élégante ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' dans l'estuaire de la Seine : historique de l'hivernage et de la nidification |trans-title=The Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in the Seine Estuary: History of Wintering and Nesting |journal=Alauda |volume=75 |pages=49-55}}</ref> and in the Mediterranean, primarily in Camargue and [[Montpellier]] ponds.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2" /> Only about ten French sites exceed international significance thresholds (730 individuals for the Atlantic, 470 for the Mediterranean).<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2" /> It is occasionally seen inland in the northwest, particularly the Loire Valley, during late migrations in December.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2" />

During breeding, it occurs in three main regions. The first is along the English Channel coast, with colonies in [[Normandy]] (about 300 pairs), Somme Bay (about 170 pairs), [[Nord-Pas-de-Calais]], and inland in [[Nord (French department)|Nord]] and [[Aisne (department)|Aisne]].<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2" />

The second region is the Atlantic coast, from [[Gulf of Morbihan]] to the [[Gironde Estuary]]. Vendée hosts the largest population with {{formatnum:1300}} pairs (30% of the national breeding population), followed by [[Loire-Atlantique]] with 500 pairs and [[Charente-Maritime]] with 400 pairs.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2" />

The third region is the Mediterranean coast, from [[La Palme]] salt flats in [[Aude (department)|Aude]] to the [[Étang de Berre]] in [[Bouches-du-Rhône]]. An isolated population exists at Hyères Salt Flats in [[Var (department)|Var]].<ref name="avocettehyeres" /> [[Bouches-du-Rhône]] hosts 550 pairs, Aude 400 pairs.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance2" /> [[Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur]] accounted for about 20% of France’s breeding population from 2003–2012. The species is absent from [[Corsica]].<ref name="vanderyeught">{{cite journal |last=Van der Yeught |first=A. |year=2013 |title=Statut de l’Avocette élégante (''Recurvirostra avosetta'') en région Provence-Alpes-Côte d’Azur |trans-title=Status of the Pied Avocet (''Recurvirostra avosetta'') in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d’Azur Region |url=https://cdnfiles1.biolovision.net/www.faune-paca.org/userfiles/FPPubli/FPP27.pdf |journal=Faune-PACA Publication |issue=27 |pages=15 |access-date=October 29, 2025}}</ref>

=== In Belgium ===
The species is distributed in the Zwin Nature Park, [[Antwerp]], and [[Ghent]], with over 480 breeding pairs.<ref name="geroudet5" />

=== In Britain ===
The pied avocet was [[Local extinction|extirpated]] as a breeding species in Great Britain by 1840.<ref name = "BBC">{{cite web |title = Birds return after 200 year gap | url = http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/uk_news/england/tees/7455668.stm | date = 15 June 2008 | publisher = [[BBC]] | access-date = 8 January 2026}}</ref> Its successful recolonisation at [[RSPB Minsmere|Minsmere]], Suffolk, in 1947 led to its adoption as the logo of the [[Royal Society for the Protection of Birds]].<ref name="rspb-history">{{cite web |title=RSPB History: From Humble Beginnings to Thriving Today |url=https://www.rspb.org.uk/about-us/our-history |publisher=[[Royal Society for the Protection of Birds]] |access-date=8 January 2026 |ref=rspb-history |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260101013452/https://www.rspb.org.uk/about-us/our-history |archive-date=1 January 2026 |url-status=live}}</ref> The pied avocet has spread inland and northwards and westwards in Britain since then and it has bred in [[Wales]]<ref name="NRW">{{cite web | url = https://naturalresources.wales/days-out/places-to-visit/south-east-wales/newport-wetlands-national-nature-reserve/?lang=en | title = Newport Wetlands National Nature Reserve | access-date = 21 July 2018 | publisher = Natural Resources Wales}}</ref> and in Scotland in 2018 at [[Skinflats]].<ref name = RSPBScotland>{{cite web | url = http://ww2.rspb.org.uk/community/ourwork/b/loch-lomond-and-black-devon-wetlands/archive/2018/07/10/rspb-39-s-emblem-bird-the-avocet-breeds-at-rspb-skinflats-for-the-first-time.aspx | title = RSPB's emblem bird, the avocet, breeds at RSPB Skinflats for the first time | access-date = 21 July 2018 | publisher = [[Royal Society for the Protection of Birds]] | archive-date = 2018-07-22 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20180722041205/http://ww2.rspb.org.uk/community/ourwork/b/loch-lomond-and-black-devon-wetlands/archive/2018/07/10/rspb-39-s-emblem-bird-the-avocet-breeds-at-rspb-skinflats-for-the-first-time.aspx }}</ref> 

== Nomenclature and taxonomy ==

=== Etymology ===
The term "avocet" derives from the old Italian ''avosetta'', attested in the 17th century, of unknown origin.<ref name="nomoiseaux">{{cite book |last1=Avenas |first1=Pierre |title=La mystérieuse histoire du nom des oiseaux |last2=Walter |first2=Henriette |publisher=Groupe Robert Laffont |year=2010 |isbn=978-2-221-11286-1 |page=322 |trans-title=The Mysterious History of Bird Names}}</ref> In French, "avocette" stems from a 1760 error by [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson|Brisson]], who replaced the "s" with a "c" in his bilingual Latin-French ''Ornithologie''. This spelling was adopted by [[Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon|Buffon]] and standardized across languages.<ref name="nomoiseaux" />

The Latin name ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' derives from ''recurvus'' ("curved") and ''rostrum'' ("beak").<ref name="nomoiseaux" />

=== Taxonomy ===
The pied avocet was one of the many [[Aves in the 10th edition of Systema Naturae|bird species originally described]] by Carl Linnaeus in his landmark 1758 [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|10th edition of ''Systema Naturae'']], where it was given the binomial name of ''Recurvirostra avosetta''.<ref>{{cite book |last=Linnaeus |first=Carl |author-link=Carl Linnaeus |url=https://archive.org/stream/carolilinnaeisy00gesegoog#page/n159/mode/1up |title=Systema Naturae per Regna Tria Naturae, Secundum Classes, Ordines, Genera, Species, cum Characteribus, Differentiis, Synonymis, Locis |publisher=(Laurentii Salvii) |year=1758 |edition=10th revised |volume=I |location=[[Stockholm|Holmiae]] |page=151 |language=la |via=The [[Internet Archive]]}}</ref> This species gets its English and scientific names from the Venetian word ''avosetta''. It appeared first in [[Ulisse Aldrovandi]]'s ''Ornithologia'' (1603). While the name may refer to black and white outfits once worn by European advocates or lawyers, the actual etymology is uncertain. Other common names include black-capped avocet, Eurasian avocet or just avocet.

It is one of four species of avocet that make up the genus ''Recurvirostra''.<ref>{{cite book |author1=Sibley, Charles Gald |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Wk-vyrNVAccC&q=Avocet+americana+novaehollandiae&pg=PA246 |title=Distribution and Taxonomy of Birds of the World |author2=Monroe, Burt Leavelle |publisher=Yale University Press |year=1990 |isbn=0-300-04969-2 |page=246}}</ref> The genus name is from Latin ''recurvus'', "curved backwards" and ''rostrum'', "bill".<ref name="job">{{cite book |last=Jobling |first=James A. |url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling |title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names |publisher=Christopher Helm |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |location=London |page=[https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n266 266]}}</ref> A 2004 study combining genetics and morphology showed that it was the most divergent species in the genus.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Thomas |first1=Gavin H. |last2=Wills |first2=Matthew A. |last3=Székely |first3=Tamás |year=2004 |title=A supertree approach to shorebird phylogeny |journal=[[BMC Evolutionary Biology]] |volume=4 |doi=10.1186/1471-2148-4-28 |pmc=515296 |pmid=15329156 |doi-access=free |article-number=28}} [https://archive.today/20130802041856/http://www.pubmedcentral.org/articlerender.fcgi?artid=515296%23supplementary-material-sec Supplementary Material]</ref>

=== Phylogeny ===
The [[mitochondrial genome]] is {{formatnum:16856}} [[Base pair|base pairs]] long.<ref name="phylogenie">{{cite journal |last1=Yang |first1=C. |last2=Wang |first2=Q. X. |last3=Li |first3=X. J. |last4=Xiao |first4=H. |last5=Huang |first5=Y. |year=2017 |title=Characterization of the mitogenomes for two sympatric breeding species in Recurvirostridae (Charadriiformes) and their phylogenetic implications |journal=Mitochondrial DNA Part B |volume=2 |issue=1 |pages=182-184}}</ref>

=== Fossil record ===
The species is recorded from the Middle and Late [[Pleistocene]] in the Mediterranean region, specifically in Sicily.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mourer-Chauviré |first=C |last2=Louchart |first2=A |last3=Pavia |first3=M |last4=Seguí |first4=B |date=2001 |title=Les avifaunes du Pléistocène moyen et supérieur des îles méditerranéennes |trans-title=The avifauna of the Middle and Upper Pleistocene of the Mediterranean islands |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/236156722_Les_avifaunes_du_Pleistocene_moyen_et_superieur_des_iles_mediterraneennes |journal=Bulletin de la Société des Sciences historiques et naturelles de la Corse |language=FR |pages=223-244}}</ref> It is also reported in northeastern Bulgaria, along the Black Sea coast, at the Yaylata site, in a deposit dating to the mid-[[Holocene]], alongside other bird species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Boev |first=Z |date=2018 |title=Fossil and subfossil record of vertebrate animals (Vertebrata J.-B. Lamarck, 1801) along the Western Black Sea Coast (Bulgaria) |url=https://acta-zoologica-bulgarica.eu/downloads/acta-zoologica-bulgarica/2018/supplement-11-105-110.pdf |journal=Proceedings of the First European Symposium “Research, Conservation and Management of Biodiversity of European Seashores |volume=11 |pages=105-110}}</ref>

== Threats and conservation ==

=== Population size and demographic trends ===
The global population was estimated to be between 280,000 and 470,000 individuals in 2015. The overall population trend is unknown. In Europe, the species' population is estimated at between 117,000 and 149,000 adult individuals in 2015, equivalent to 58,400 to 74,300 breeding pairs, or, according to another source, between 38,000 and 57,000 pairs.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3">{{cite book |last1=Issa |first1=Nidal |title=Atlas des oiseaux de France métropolitaine |last2=Muller |first2=Yves |publisher=Delachaux et Niestlé |year=2015 |isbn=978-2-603-01878-1 |location=Paris |page=498-501 |trans-title=Atlas of Breeding and Wintering Birds of Metropolitan France}}</ref>

The Western European breeding population is estimated at 73,000 birds, wintering across Europe and Africa, from the Netherlands to northern [[Guinea]], with 22,500 individuals in [[Sub-Saharan Africa]].<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> France accounts for 13% of the European breeding population. During the 20th century, the species experienced a significant population increase in Europe, coupled with substantial geographic expansion, which began to stabilize in the early 21st century.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" />

In France, between 2002 and 2006, an average of 20,800 wintering birds were observed, with significant interannual variations due to weather, with harsher winters reducing numbers. Between 2010 and 2013, the French wintering population ranged between 17,000 and 25,000 individuals,<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" /> representing nearly half of the European population.<ref name="avocettehyeres" /> Since 1977, wintering numbers in France have been increasing; between 1980 and 2013, the wintering population grew by 49%, stabilizing between 2011 and 2013.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

Locally, there are marked differences between wintering sites. For example, along the English Channel coast, numbers have declined sharply by 80% since 1980, largely due to reduced habitat capacity and feeding areas at the [[Seine Bay]], following the development of the [[Port of Le Havre]].<ref name="avocetteseine2">{{cite journal |last1=Aulert |first1=C. |last2=Hémery |first2=D. |year=2007 |title=L'Avocette élégante ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' dans l'estuaire de la Seine : historique de l'hivernage et de la nidification |trans-title=The Pied Avocet ''Recurvirostra avosetta'' in the Seine Estuary: History of Wintering and Nesting |journal=Alauda |volume=75 |pages=49-55}}</ref> In contrast, along the Atlantic coast, numbers increased by 107% since 1980. The Mediterranean wintering population has also risen by 200% since 1980, though this is tempered by strong interannual variations.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" />

During the breeding season, the species is relatively localized, with 3,650 to 4,350 breeding pairs recorded in 2010–2011,<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" /> including 250 pairs along the English Channel and 1,500 to 2,500 pairs along the Atlantic coast in 2004.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> In the Mediterranean region, between 1991 and 1999, 810 to 928 pairs were recorded.<ref name="repromedit2">{{cite journal |last1=Sadoul |first1=N. |last2=Cramm |first2=P. |last3=Orsini |first3=P. |year=2005 |title=Population trends, reproduction and conservation issues of the Avocet breeding on the Mediterranean coast of France |journal=Wader Study Group Bulletin |volume=107 |pages=94-97}}</ref> Between 1996 and 2011, the Mediterranean breeding population increased by over 50%, reaching approximately 1,500 pairs.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" />

Along the Atlantic and English Channel coasts, the breeding population has significantly increased since the establishment of new colonies in the 1970s; since 1996, numbers have doubled, accounting for 57% of France's total breeding population.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" /> In the Mediterranean, breeding numbers are generally stable, with notable variations by site (increases in Languedoc and a sharp decline in the Rhône Delta).<ref name="repromedit2" /> However, between 1997 and 2007, the Mediterranean population was considered to be declining on its wintering grounds and migratory routes.<ref name="AtlasOiseauxFrance3" />

=== Conservation status ===
Due to its extensive global range (over {{cvt|20,000|km2}}) and large population size, the species is not considered threatened and has been classified as least concern (LC) by the IUCN since 2004.<ref name="iucn"/> In Europe, its conservation status is deemed favorable.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> In France, it is also classified as least concern (LC) as both a breeding and wintering species. As a migratory species, it is classified as not applicable (NA) due to insufficient data.<ref>{{Cite web |last=UICN France |last2=MNHN |last3=LPO |last4=ONCFS |date=2016 |title=La Liste rouge des espèces menacées en France - Chapitre Oiseaux de France métropolitaine |trans-title=The Red List of Threatened Species in France - Chapter: Birds of Metropolitan France |url=https://inpn.mnhn.fr/docs/LR_FCE/UICN-LR-Oiseaux-diffusion.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250521174925/https://inpn.mnhn.fr/docs/LR_FCE/UICN-LR-Oiseaux-diffusion.pdf#federation=archive.wikiwix.com&tab=url |archive-date=21 May 2025 |website=Liste rouge des espèces menacées |language=fr |access-date=2 November 2025 |url-status=dead }}</ref>

The species is considered vulnerable (VU) as a breeding species on the regional red list of Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur,<ref>{{Cite journal |date=2012 |title=Liste rouge régionale des oiseaux nicheurs de Provence-Alpes-Côte d’Azur. |trans-title=Regional Red List of breeding birds in Provence-Alpes-Côte d’Azur. |url=https://www.paca.developpement-durable.gouv.fr/IMG/pdf/CENPACA_ListeRougeOISEAUX_web_erratum.pdf |journal=Liste rouge des espèces menacées |language=fr |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251223195237/https://www.paca.developpement-durable.gouv.fr/IMG/pdf/CENPACA_ListeRougeOISEAUX_web_erratum.pdf |archive-date=23 December 2025 |access-date=11 December 2025 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> near threatened (NT) on the red list of breeding birds in [[Languedoc-Roussillon]], vulnerable (VU) in [[Poitou-Charentes]], and least concern (LC) in [[Pays de la Loire]]. In [[Brittany]], it is vulnerable (VU) as a breeding species and near threatened (NT) as a migrant. In Normandy, it is critically endangered (CR) as a breeding species and endangered (EN) as a wintering species. In Nord-Pas-de-Calais, it is vulnerable (VU) as a breeding species.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Beaudoin |first=C |last2=Camberlein |first2=P |title=Liste rouge des Oiseaux nicheurs du Nord–Pas-de-Calais |trans-title=Red List of Breeding Birds in Nord–Pas-de-Calais |url=https://gon.fr/gon/wp-content/uploads/2017/12/gon-liste-rouge-des-oiseaux-nicheurs-du-nord-pas-de-calais.pdf |journal=Liste rouge des espèces menacées |language=fr |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200702162436/https://gon.fr/gon/wp-content/uploads/2017/12/gon-liste-rouge-des-oiseaux-nicheurs-du-nord-pas-de-calais.pdf |archive-date=2 July 2020}}</ref> In Belgium, specifically [[Wallonia]], it is considered vulnerable (VU).<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Paquet |first=J. Y. |last2=Jacob |first2=J. P. |date=2010 |title=Liste rouge 2010 des oiseaux nicheurs de Wallonie |trans-title=2010 Red List of breeding birds in Wallonia |journal=Liste rouge des espèces menacées |language=FR}}</ref>

=== Threats ===
[[File:Erika_littoral_pollution_map-fr.svg|alt=Map showing the impact of the Erika oil spill on the French Atlantic coast.|thumb|Pollution (oil spill) from the Erika on French coasts during the winter of 1999–2000.]]
Various threats, mostly of [[Human impact on the environment|anthropogenic origin]], affect the Pied Avocet. The European population is relatively small and confined to a limited number of sites, particularly in winter.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Hötker |first=H |last2=West |first2=R |date=2005 |title=Population size, population development and habitat use by Avocets in western Europe at the end of the 20th century |journal=Wader Study Group Bulletin |volume=107 |pages=57-65}}</ref> Many key wintering sites are located near oil port facilities, posing a significant petrochemical risk, as evidenced by a collision between two butane carrier ships in January 2006 in the Loire Estuary.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> The risk of [[oil spills]], such as the ecological disaster caused by the [[Erika (tanker)|Erika]] in 1999–2000, also led to a decline in French wintering populations.<ref name=":0" />

Climate change has a notable impact on the species' reproductive success and affects its coastal habitat.<ref name=":0" />

Habitat loss, particularly due to increasing coastal [[urbanization]], is a major issue.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name="avocettehyeres" /> The degradation and destruction of coastal wetlands, essential for breeding, lead to more frequent disturbances, affecting reproduction, rest, and feeding by reducing available feeding areas for both chicks and wintering birds.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

Intensified agricultural practices have caused significant drying of marshes, with, for example, {{convert|40,000|ha}} of [[wet meadow]]s destroyed in the [[Marais Poitevin]]. Poor water management, with excessive water level fluctuations, can be disastrous for colonies, as seen in the Somme Bay.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> Additionally, the abandonment of traditional practices in salt marshes and exploited plots alters the species' characteristic habitat and associated vegetation, negatively impacting colonies.<ref name="avocettehyeres" />

Specific urbanization examples include port developments, such as in France’s Seine Estuary<ref name="avocetteseine2" /> and Loire Estuary, where mudflat areas have decreased by 25% in just 20 years. Excessive urbanization in Portugal and the [[Yellow Sea]] also poses problems.<ref name="delhoyo3">{{cite book |last1=del Hoyo |first1=J. |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World, vol. 3 |last2=Elliott |first2=A. |last3=Sargatal |first3=J. |publisher=Lynx Edicions |year=1996 |isbn=978-84-87334-20-7 |pages=821}}</ref> The species may also be affected by reduced river flows in certain areas, such as in China.

Pollution is harmful to the Pied Avocet, with pollutants such as [[Polychlorinated biphenyl|PCBs]], [[insecticides]], [[lead]], [[Mercury (element)|mercury]], and [[selenium]] found in eggs and bird tissues in Europe.<ref name="delhoyo3" />

Locally, [[hunting]] and [[mosquito control]] can disturb the species and disrupt or destroy favorable [[biotopes]].<ref name="avocettehyeres" /> The species is susceptible to diseases such as [[avian botulism]] and [[avian influenza]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Melville |first=D. S. |last2=Shortridge |first2=K. F. |date=2006 |chapter=Waterbirds and avian influenza in the East Asian-Australasian Flyway with particular reference to the 2003-2004 H5N1 outbreak |title=Waterbirds around the world |pages=432-438 |editor1=Boere. G.C. |editor2=Galbraith, C.A. |editor3=Stroud, D.A. |publisher=TSO Scotland Ltd |location=Edinburgh, UK |url=https://jncc.gov.uk/resources/08cfb4da-4c5a-4bef-b45d-8f2f87dc8070 }}</ref>

Finally, the species may occasionally be affected by egg collection for private collections.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mouronval |first=J. B. |last2=Triplet |first2=P |date=1991 |title=Oiseaux d'eau nicheurs en Plaine Maritime Picarde |trans-title=Breeding waterbirds in the Picardy Maritime Plain |journal=Conseil Régional de Picardie |language=FR}}</ref>

=== Conservation measures ===
[[File:Bagues_avocette.jpg|alt=Image showing bird leg rings, one blue with a white code C51 and two metal rings with unique codes placed on cardboard.|left|thumb|Metal and Darvic rings used for banding an avocet chick at Salins-d'Hyères (Var).]]
The species is protected in France under the Nature Protection Law of July 10, 1976, and its implementing decrees. The European Commission’s 1979 [[Birds Directive]] and the 1979 [[Berne Convention on the Conservation of European Wildlife and Natural Habitats|Bern Convention]] on European wildlife ensure full legal protection, prohibiting disturbance of birds and their nests.<ref name="avocettehyeres" /><ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /> The species is listed in Annex I of the Birds Directive, Annex II of the Bern Convention, Annex II of the [[Bonn Convention]], and Category B1 of the [[African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbird Agreement]] (AEWA).<ref name="avocettehyeres" /><ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" />

The establishment of [[Nature reserve|nature reserves]], [[Marine protected area|marine reserves]], and [[Special Protection Area|special protection areas]] (SPAs) has benefited the Western European population during the 20th century.<ref name="INPNCahierHabitatOiseaux" /><ref name=":0" />

Various management measures can promote settlement and reproduction. Constructing artificial nesting sites in coastal areas, such as pebble beaches or islands and rafts with sparse vegetation, can attract breeding pairs.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Burgess |first=N. D. |last2=Hirons |first2=J. M. |date=1992 |title=Creation and management of articficial nesting sites for wetland birds |journal=Journal of Environmental Management |volume=34 |pages=285-295}}</ref> Increased breeding pairs have also been noted with the introduction of livestock grazing in coastal meadows near breeding sites, reducing vegetation cover and improving visibility and predator detection.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Olsen |first=H. |last2=Schmidt |first2=N. M. |date=2004 |title=Impacts of wet grassland management and winter severity on wader breeding numbers in eastern Denmark |journal=Basic and Applied Ecology |volume=5 |pages=203-210}}</ref>

Numerous color-banding programs, using metal and colored rings, exist in Europe, enhancing ornithologists’ understanding of the species’ ecology and migratory movements, as well as improving monitoring of breeding behavior and site management.<ref name="avocettehyeres" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=Find a colour-ring project |url=https://cr-birding.org/colourprojects?tid_3=&tid_2=Recurvirostra+avosetta&tid=All&tid_1=All&tid_4=All&tid_5=All |website=European colour-ring birding}}</ref>

zh.extract:
反嘴鷸（yù）（学名：Recurvirostra avosetta），又称反嘴鴴（héng）、反嘴長腳鷸，为反嘴鹬科反嘴鷸屬的鸟类。这物种分布在欧洲，西亚和中亚的温带地区。这是一个迁徙物种，大部分冬季迁徙至非洲或亚洲南部。部分冬季停留在分布地区最温暖的区域，例如西班牙南部和英格兰南部。在中国大陆，分布于华北和华南等地。该物种的模式产地在意大利。反嘴鷸是非歐亞遷移性水鳥協定（AEWA）適用的物種之一。


分類
反嘴鷸是由卡爾·林奈在他1758年的《自然系統》第10版中首次描述的眾多鳥類物種之一，當時牠被賦予了雙名法的學名Recurvirostra avosetta.。該物種的英語名稱和學名來自威尼斯語單詞avosetta。該名稱首次出現在烏利塞·阿爾德羅萬迪的《鳥類學》（Ornithologia） （1603） 中。 雖然該名稱可能與歐洲律師或法律代表曾經穿著的黑白服裝有關，但實際的辭源學尚不確定。 
這是組成反嘴鷸屬（Recurvirostra）.的四個反嘴鷸物種之一。屬名來自拉丁語recurvus，意為「向後彎曲」和rostrum，意為「鳥喙」。 2004年結合基因和形態學的研究顯示，牠是該屬中最具差異性的物種}>


描述
反嘴鷸是一種引人注目的白色涉禽，具有鮮明的黑色斑紋。成鳥擁有白色的羽衣，只有頭頂有黑色的帽子，翅膀和背部有黑色的斑塊。牠們有長長的向上翹起的喙和藍色的長腿。體長約16.5—17.75英寸（41.9—45.1 cm），其中喙長約2.95—3.35英寸（7.5—8.5 cm），腿長約3—4英寸（7.6—10.2 cm），翼展約30—31.5英寸（76—80 cm）。雄性和雌性外觀相似。幼鳥與成鳥相似，但帶有更多灰色和赭色的色調。
反嘴鷸的叫聲是遠距離傳播的、流動的、悅耳的kluit kluit.


行為
這些鳥類在淺鹹水或泥灘上覓食，常常在水中從一側到另一側掃動牠們的喙（這是一種只有反嘴鷸採用的獨特覓食技術）。牠們主要以甲殼類和昆蟲為食。
牠們的繁殖棲地是帶有鹹水和裸露泥土的淺湖。牠們在空曠地面上築巢，經常成群繁殖，有時與其他涉禽共同繁殖。牠們在襯墊的坑洞或植被堆上產下三到五枚鳥蛋。


在英國
反嘴鷸作為繁殖物種在1840年於英國局部滅絕。 牠們在1947年於薩福克郡的Minsmere成功重建繁殖群體，並因此被選為英國皇家鳥類保護協會的標誌。 自那時以來，反嘴鷸在英國內陸及北方和西方擴展，並且已經在威爾士和2018年在蘇格蘭福爾柯克的Skinflats繁殖。 冬季時在蘇格蘭鄧弗里斯-加洛韋的Browhouses亦有觀察到反嘴鷸。
